content:
Technology

AI

Artificial Intelligence

Programming

AI Agent

I read the headline on Friday, nodded, and scrolled. That’s the part I want to talk about. Not the headline. The nod.

5 min read
Just now

--

A Derbyshire police officer is under criminal investigation for allegedly using AI to create evidential material in a number of cases.
That’s close to the actual phrasing from the constabulary, the alleged use of AI systems by an officer to create evidential material.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The officer’s been pulled off frontline duty, nobody’s been named, nobody’s in custody, it’s early days. The Financial Times had it first, last Friday. People who follow this stuff think it’s the first case of its kind in the UK criminal justice system.
The Crown Prosecution Service is now going back through cases to work out which ones might be poisoned.
And my reaction, the honest one, was well, yeah. Of course that happened. Of course it was going to. I build AI features for a living and I felt zero surprise, and the lack of surprise is the whole story.

The thing I keep circling
For basically all of recorded history, faking a convincing artifact was expensive. A forged document took a skilled forger. A doctored photo took a darkroom and talent, then Photoshop and talent, then at least a lost afternoon.
The cost of making something that looks real was the quiet tax that kept looks real and is real roughly in the same place. Not perfectly. But close enough that a court, a newsroom, a manager, a tired code reviewer could lean on it most of the time and be mostly right.
We spent the last three years driving that cost to zero. And we cheered while we did it, me included.
I’ve shipped the features. A thing that generates a plausible summary of a document nobody read. A thing that spits out a clean, official-looking PDF from five form fields.
I’ve sat in the demo and watched a room go ooh at output that looked exactly like a human spent an hour on it, and the entire selling point was that no human did. That’s the product. Looks authentic, wasn’t.
The flip side of anyone can make something that looks real for free is nothing that looks real proves anything anymore. It’s the same sentence. We only ever sold the first half of it.

The boring version is the scary one
The deepfake conversation always gets stuck on the spectacular stuff. A fake video of a president.
A cloned voice of a CEO wiring eight figures somewhere. Big, cinematic, and rare enough that most people picture a sophisticated operator and quietly feel safe, because they aren’t a head of state.
This Derbyshire thing is the other version. An ordinary officer, ordinary cases, allegedly producing evidential material the way you or I would generate a Jira description we couldn’t be bothered to write by hand.
No movie-villain budget. No special access. The same tab everyone already has open, pointed at something that’s supposed to be true.
That’s the version that actually rewires things, because it scales to everyone and it lives in the mundane. Nobody fact-checks the unremarkable. A screenshot in a report.
A log line someone pasted into a post-mortem. The transcript stapled to a complaint that everyone takes at face value because reading it twice was never worth anyone’s time.
We wave that stuff through all day, and “it looks like a normal screenshot” used to be a fine reason to relax. It isn’t now. It just still feels like one, which is worse.

To be fair to the industry
There is an answer to this and I don’t want to be cheap about it. It’s provenance, cryptographically signing content at the moment it’s captured so you can later prove where it came from. C2PA, Content Credentials, the little made with a camera or made with AI manifest that rides along inside the file.
Adobe, a bunch of camera makers, some of the big AI shops are behind it. It’s real, and it’s good engineering.
It’s also nowhere near how anything actually works yet. Almost nothing you looked at today was signed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A screenshot strips the metadata on its way out. A signature can tell you a photo came from a genuine camera and tell you nothing about whether the scene in front of that camera was staged for it.
And the sergeant at 11pm, the hiring manager, the duty solicitor, none of them are inspecting a content credential.
We built a civilization that runs on unsigned, unverifiable artifacts, then made those artifacts free to fabricate, and the fix is a standard most people have never heard the name of.

Where I do it too
So I went looking for where I personally lean on “looks real, so it’s real,” since it’s easy to stand over a police officer and tut.
It’s all over how I work. I trust the screenshots in bug reports. I trust the logs a service hands me without once wondering whether something upstream could have shaped them before I saw them.
I have pasted AI-written text into a ticket and let a teammate assume I typed it, which is a tiny, harmless cousin of the exact move, presenting generated material as the genuine human article because it was faster and it sailed through.
We have no chain of custody for any of the digital stuff we make real decisions on. We never built one. We didn’t need one, because forging it was a hassle.
It stopped being a hassle.
I don’t have a clean fix, which is probably why this has been sitting in my head for three days instead of turning into a tidy take. “Just verify things” is useless advice when the entire reason the artifact existed was that verifying it wasn’t worth the minute.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The honest version is smaller and more annoying: the default has to flip. It looks legit, so it probably is was a reasonable bet for a couple of centuries, and it quietly stopped paying out, and most of us, courts very much included, never got the memo.
Somebody allegedly worked that out before the rest of us did, and used it. That’s the part I can’t get past. Not the crime. The shrug I gave it.
Anyway. Next time a screenshot decides something at your job, notice that you believed it on sight. I did, this morning, twice, before I caught myself.
